_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The ACT regulation places direct compliance obligations on **truck manufacturers** (OEMs certifying Class 2b–8 vehicles with combustion engines for California sale). The workspace, as a freight forwarder, is not a manufacturer and is not a direct compliance obligor under ACT.

The workspace's position in the compliance chain:

| Layer | Entity | ACT obligation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — Manufacturer | OEM certifying Class 2b–8 trucks | Direct: ZEV sales percentage targets by model year |
| 2 — Fleet operator / carrier | Drayage and over-the-road carriers used by the workspace | Direct under ACF (companion regulation); indirect buyer of ZEV-compliant stock under ACT |
| 3 — Freight forwarder (the workspace) | Procures carrier services; does not own fleet | No direct ACT obligation; exposed through carrier availability and pricing |
| 4 — Shipper / client | Beneficial cargo owner | No direct ACT obligation; exposed through forwarder's carrier procurement |

**Legal Confirmation Required:** Whether the workspace's ACF-defined "large entity" one-time reporting obligation applies depends on fleet ownership and operational control thresholds defined in the ACF regulation, not ACT. If the workspace does not own or operate a truck fleet in California, it is likely outside the ACF reporting scope, but this determination requires legal review against the ACF regulation text and the workspace's specific operational arrangements. This document covers ACT only; ACF analysis is a separate instrument.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page (distinguishes manufacturer mandate from fleet reporting). https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks*

---
